us-politics.headline: US Forces Take Hits as Iran Conflict Escalates
us-politics.body:
American fighter jets and two helicopters have been struck during operations connected to the Iran conflict, with recovery efforts coming under fire. The incidents mark a serious escalation in US military exposure in the region. World leaders are meanwhile moving to address Strait of Hormuz tensions without waiting for Washington, raising questions about American influence over the crisis.

The bypassing of Trump by allied governments signals growing unease with US leadership on the conflict. Details on casualties and the full scope of damage to aircraft remain limited, but the losses represent a significant operational setback. Congress has yet to weigh in formally.

sources.title: World leaders bypass Trump to tackle Strait of Hormuz crisis
What to know about US fighter jets hit in Iran war
2 US helicopters hit during recovery efforts in Iran

us-politics.headline: Cabinet Shake-Up Continues as Trump Eyes More Changes
us-politics.body:
The departures of Pam Bondi and Kristi Noem from the Cabinet have fuelled speculation about further reshuffles, though Trump is said to want to avoid a wholesale overhaul. Todd Blanche, Trump's former personal attorney, has taken the top job at the Justice Department, stepping into a role freighted with political expectations from the president.

Reports suggest Trump is weighing moves involving Commerce Secretary Lutnick and others, but advisers are urging caution. The rapid turnover has drawn scrutiny over stability and loyalty tests inside the administration. Blanche's appointment in particular draws attention given his prior role defending Trump in criminal proceedings.

sources.title: Bondi, Noem ousters raise questions about future Cabinet shake-ups
Trump weighs more Cabinet changes, but wants to avoid ‘massive shake-up’
Todd Blanche takes on DOJ’s top job and the burden of Trump’s expectations

us-politics.headline: Trump Acts on College Sports, Day Care and Voting Rights
us-politics.body:
Trump signed an executive order targeting college sports governance, his second such directive aimed at reshaping NCAA rules around athlete compensation and eligibility. Supporters say federal intervention is overdue; critics argue it oversteps executive authority into areas traditionally left to institutions and states.

On child care, Trump floated shifting federal responsibility to states, a move advocates say would gut access for low-income families. Oregon's attorney general sued the administration over a separate order restricting vote-by-mail, calling it a source of confusion and legal chaos. The three moves together reflect a broad push to redraw federal boundaries - and are drawing sharp resistance from Democrats and advocacy groups alike.

sources.title: Trump floats letting states handle day care. Advocates call it a lead balloon
Trump signs order aimed at reforming college sports
Trump again orders changes to ‘save’ college sports
‘Confusion, chaos and distrust’: Oregon challenges Trump’s order restricting vote-by-mail

us-politics.headline: Budget Fights, Retirement Fears and a Good Friday Message
us-politics.body:
Trump's defense budget proposal is drawing mixed reactions, with Republican Senator Susan Collins publicly criticising funding cuts she says go too far. The White House is simultaneously directing DHS to continue paying employees during a partial shutdown, a move that sidesteps the usual congressional appropriations process.

Separately, a new payroll industry report warns that Americans are saving less for retirement, a trend that could become a political liability as economic anxiety rises. Trump marked Good Friday with a message declaring America needs God and pointing to what he called a resurgence of religion. The statement drew praise from conservatives and skepticism from those who see it as political signalling ahead of a holiday weekend.

sources.title: Warning sign? Retirement saving habits eroding, payroll firm says
Trump directs DHS to pay employees during shutdown
Trump says ‘America needs God’ in Good Friday message touting ‘resurgence of religion'
Susan Collins knocks funding cuts in White House budget proposal
What to know about Trump’s defense budget proposal

au-politics.headline: Drug Syndicate Busted After Trawler Sinks Off Australian Coast
au-politics.body:
Nine people face charges over an alleged plot to import tonnes of cocaine and methamphetamine into Australia using a so-called 'mother ship' operating in Australian waters. The operation unravelled after a sunken trawler alerted police to the smuggling network, triggering a major investigation.

Authorities say the syndicate had the capacity to move drugs at an industrial scale. The arrests represent one of the larger drug importation busts in recent memory. No political response has been issued yet, though the case is likely to renew debate about maritime border surveillance and organised crime enforcement.

sources.title: Nine charged over alleged conspiracy to import tonnes of cocaine and meth via ‘mother ship’ in Australian waters
Sunken trawler alerts police to alleged drug-smuggling plan

au-politics.headline: Pezzullo Report Reveals 'Reckless' Ties to Liberal Powerbroker
au-politics.body:
A confidential report has found former Home Affairs secretary Michael Pezzullo acted recklessly in his dealings with a Liberal Party powerbroker, adding fresh detail to the conduct that led to his sacking in 2023. The findings raise ongoing questions about the boundary between senior public servants and political operatives.

Pezzullo was one of Australia's most powerful bureaucrats before his dismissal. Critics argue the report confirms a culture of political entanglement at the top of the public service. Supporters have previously said he was a victim of selective leaking and political manoeuvring. The government has not publicly commented on the confidential findings.

sources.title: Confidential report found former home affairs boss Michael Pezzullo was ‘reckless’ in engagement with Liberal powerbroker

au-politics.headline: Fuel Shortage Fears Ease but Cost Pressures Hit Farmers Hard
au-politics.body:
Energy Minister Chris Bowen says fewer service stations are running dry as Australia's fuel supply stabilises following recent disruption. However, the crisis has left a financial hangover - farmers are calling on supermarkets to absorb higher transport costs rather than pass them down the supply chain.

Agricultural groups argue that without relief, rising fuel costs will squeeze already thin margins and ultimately push food prices higher. Supermarkets have not publicly committed to any change in pricing arrangements. The Spectator has separately criticised what it calls 'parasite politics' around energy policy, arguing government intervention distorts markets rather than solving supply problems.

sources.title: Fewer service stations running out of petrol and diesel as Australia’s fuel supplies remain strong, energy minister says
Supermarkets asked to pay more for produce to absorb fuel costs for farmers
The West’s parasite politics

au-politics.headline: Victorian Politics Heats Up as Lethlean Targets Premier's Seat
au-politics.body:
Andrew Lethlean has confirmed he will contest Bendigo East at the next Victorian state election, directly challenging Premier Jacinta Allan in her own electorate. The move signals an opposition intent to put Allan on the defensive at home rather than cede the seat.

Bendigo East has been a safe Labor seat, but challengers targeting sitting premiers can shift local dynamics and force leaders to spend campaign resources defending their own turf. Allan has not yet publicly responded to Lethlean's announcement. The Victorian election is due later in 2026.

sources.title: Lethlean to challenge Victorian premier in Bendigo East

world.headline: US-Israel War on Iran Enters Day 36 With Escalating Strikes
world.body:
US and Israeli forces continued strikes on Iran for a 36th consecutive day, with Tehran claiming it shot down two American warplanes and fired missiles that hit residential areas in central Israel. Israel simultaneously bombed bridges in Lebanon, widening the conflict's geographic footprint. A rescue team in Iran is conducting what officials describe as a harrowing search for a missing US crew member, adding a new dimension to the crisis.

Iran executed two members of a banned opposition group amid the fighting, a move critics say is designed to suppress dissent under cover of wartime. The conflict is reshaping the region rapidly, with no ceasefire in sight.

sources.title: Rescue team in Iran face 'harrowing and dangerous' search for US crew member
Iran executes two convicted members of banned opposition group
Iran war: What is happening on day 36 of US-Israeli attacks?
Iranian missile attack hits residential areas in central Israel
Iran war live: Tehran downs 2 US warplanes; Israel bombs Lebanon bridges

world.headline: Russia Kills Four in Ukraine Over Easter Weekend
world.body:
Russian forces killed at least four people in Ukraine over the Easter period, with drone footage capturing the moment one strike hit a residential apartment block. Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky accused Russia of deliberately choosing escalation over a ceasefire during the holiday, framing the attacks as a calculated rejection of any pause in hostilities.

The strikes follow a pattern of targeting civilian infrastructure. Zelensky's comments signal deepening frustration with international efforts to broker even a temporary halt to the fighting.

sources.title: At least four people killed in Russian attacks on Ukraine
Russia chose 'Easter escalation' over ceasefire, says Zelensky
Video shows the moment a Russian drone hit an apartment in Ukraine

world.headline: Artemis II Crew Reaches Halfway Point to Moon
world.body:
NASA's Artemis II crew passed the halfway mark of their lunar journey, releasing striking images of Earth taken from deep space. The mission marks the first crewed flight beyond low Earth orbit in over five decades, and the photographs have drawn widespread attention for their scale and clarity.

The milestone comes as NASA pushes to re-establish a sustained human presence near the Moon. The crew is expected to loop around the Moon before returning to Earth.

sources.title: Artemis II crew now halfway to Moon as they take 'spectacular' image of Earth
NASA unveils first images of Earth from Artemis II

world.headline: Trump Moves to Reopen Alcatraz and Drill Federal Wilderness
world.body:
President Trump requested $152 million to reopen Alcatraz as a functioning federal prison, reviving a facility that closed in 1963. The proposal is widely seen as a political signal on crime and punishment rather than a practical corrections policy, given the island's extreme operating costs.

Separately, Trump moved to open protected wilderness areas to oil and gas drilling, advancing his administration's energy agenda. Europe, meanwhile, is debating a nuclear revival of its own as energy prices climb, with Senegal going further by banning ministers from foreign travel to cut government spending amid the global oil price squeeze.

sources.title: Trump seeks $152m to reopen notorious Alcatraz prison
'Drill baby drill': Trump opens wilderness to big energy
Faced with new energy shock, Europe asks if reviving nuclear is the answer
Senegal bans ministers from foreign travel as oil price rise bites

ai.headline: Anthropic Expands Aggressively Across Markets, Politics, and Biotech
ai.body:
Anthropic is having an unusually busy week. The company acquired biotech startup Coefficient Bio for $400 million, launched a new political action committee, and is riding high in private markets - though analysts warn a SpaceX IPO could suck oxygen from the room.

Meanwhile, Anthropic moved to cut off OpenClaw, a third-party Claude client, by requiring subscribers to pay extra to use it. The move effectively bans the tool for most users. OpenClaw's security record isn't helping its case - Ars Technica reports users should assume their accounts have been compromised.

On the technical side, Claude Code earned genuine praise after a developer documented how it uncovered a Linux vulnerability that had sat hidden for 23 years. Anthropic's research arm also published findings on how emotion concepts function inside large language models.

sources.title: Emotion concepts and their function in a large language model
Anthropic is having a moment in the private markets; SpaceX could spoil the party
Anthropic essentially bans OpenClaw from Claude by making subscribers pay extra
Claude Code Found a Linux Vulnerability Hidden for 23 Years
Tell HN: Anthropic no longer allowing Claude Code subscriptions to use OpenClaw
OpenClaw gives users yet another reason to be freaked out about security
Anthropic buys biotech startup Coefficient Bio in $400M deal: Reports
Anthropic ramps up its political activities with a new PAC

ai.headline: OpenAI Reshuffles Leadership as Key Executives Step Back
ai.body:
OpenAI is reorganizing at the top. COO Brad Lightcap is moving into a new "special projects" role, while Fidji Simo is taking medical leave. The company's AGI lead is also stepping away. Three senior figures sidelined in quick succession is hard to read as routine.

The shake-up comes as OpenAI faces intensifying competition from Anthropic and pressure to show a credible path to profitability. What the restructuring signals about internal strategy - or internal friction - remains unclear.

sources.title: OpenAI executive shuffle includes new role for COO Brad Lightcap to lead ‘special projects’
OpenAI’s AGI boss is taking a leave of absence
OpenAI’s Fidji Simo Is Taking Medical Leave Amid an Executive Shake-Up

ai.headline: AI's Infrastructure Ambitions Run Into Tariffs, Gas Plants, and Public Backlash
ai.body:
The AI industry's data center buildout is hitting walls from multiple directions. Trump's own tariffs are undermining his administration's push for domestic AI infrastructure, driving up hardware costs and slowing construction. A separate survey found people would sooner accept an Amazon warehouse next door than a data center.

Energy is the other pressure point. AI companies are racing to build large natural gas plants to power their facilities, raising serious questions about emissions and long-term grid stability. The gap between AI's ambitions and the physical world's constraints is widening fast.

sources.title: Trump ignores biggest reasons his AI data center buildout is failing
AI companies are building huge natural gas plants to power data centers. What could go wrong?
People would rather have an Amazon warehouse in their backyard than a data center

ai.headline: Artemis II Heads to the Moon as NASA Budget Faces Cuts
ai.body:
Artemis II is in flight and performing well. The crew captured striking images of Earth, and mission controllers report no significant issues. It is the first crewed lunar mission in over 50 years.

The timing is awkward. Trump's administration simultaneously proposed steep cuts to NASA's budget, raising questions about what comes after Artemis II. Sending astronauts to the Moon while defunding the agency responsible for getting them home is a tension the White House has not resolved.

sources.title: Trump proposes steep cut to NASA budget as astronauts head for the Moon
As Artemis II zooms to the Moon, everything seems to be going swimmingly
Artemis II crew take “spectacular” image of Earth

btc.headline: Bitcoin Consolidates as Whales Bleed $337M Daily
btc.body:
Bitcoin's price action has gone quiet, but the damage underneath is real. Large traders lost an average of $337 million per day through Q1 2026, a stretch analysts now describe as directionless consolidation rather than a healthy base-build.

That sideways grind may not last. At least one analyst argues the longer Bitcoin trades without conviction, the more violent the eventual breakout tends to be - in either direction. The question is whether institutional holders can absorb further losses before that move arrives.

Meanwhile, the long-term bull case remains intact for some. An analyst tracking ETF flows argues Bitcoin funds will eventually surpass gold ETFs in size, pointing to expanding use cases and sustained institutional appetite despite the rough quarter.

sources.title: Rich Bitcoin traders lost $337M daily in first quarter of 2026
Bitcoin’s ‘no direction’ action may lead to heavier breakout: Analyst
Bitcoin ETFs 'will be larger' than gold ETFs: Analyst

btc.headline: Prediction Markets Face Courts, Backlash, and Valuation Doubts
btc.body:
Prediction markets had a rough week on multiple fronts. Polymarket pulled a market tied to a missing US pilot after public backlash, a rare retreat for a platform that has generally resisted pressure to self-censor. The episode reopens questions about where the line sits between liquid information markets and exploitation of tragedy.

In the US, Kalshi fared no better in court. A Nevada judge extended a ban on the platform's sports betting markets, keeping a key revenue category off-limits while the legal fight continues.

Tether, which has been eyeing a fundraising round at a $500 billion valuation, may now delay that process if investor demand falls short. The figure is enormous, and hesitation from the market would signal that even stablecoin dominance has limits when it comes to private capital.

sources.title: Polymarket takes down market on missing US pilot after backlash
Tether may delay fundraising if demand falls short at $500B valuation: Report
Judge continues Nevada ban on Kalshi sports markets

btc.headline: Quantum 'Bitcoin Crack' Claims Need Heavy Qualification
btc.body:
Headlines claiming quantum computers can crack Bitcoin in nine minutes are spreading fast and missing most of the context. The research describes a theoretical attack on a narrow cryptographic problem under highly specific conditions - not a live threat to the Bitcoin network as it operates today.

Current quantum hardware remains far from the scale needed to threaten Bitcoin's elliptic curve cryptography in practice. Developers have also been tracking this risk for years, and post-quantum cryptography upgrades are an active area of work. The nine-minute figure is real in a limited technical sense. As a near-term danger to Bitcoin holders, it is not.

sources.title: Here's what 'cracking' bitcoin in 9 minutes by quantum computers actually means

food.name: Miso-Glazed Leek and Mushroom Galette
food.description: A free-form savory tart where caramelized leeks and shiitake mushrooms are lacquered with white miso butter inside a shatteringly flaky rough-puff pastry.
food.tip: Freeze your butter for 20 minutes before grating it into the flour — it creates distinct flaky layers without a food processor.

exercise.name: The Saturday Staircase Pyramid
exercise.equipment: A staircase of at least 10 steps, timer
exercise.description: Climb the stairs 1 time, rest 15 seconds, then 2 times, rest 20 seconds, building up to 8 continuous climbs then descending back down the pyramid — combining cardio, leg strength, and mental grit in one compact session.

learn.topic: Eigenvalues and the Google PageRank Algorithm
learn.description: PageRank — the algorithm that made Google — is essentially a problem in linear algebra: the ranking of every webpage on the internet is the principal eigenvector of a massive matrix representing links between pages.
learn.why: Understanding this collapses the mystery of how a mathematical abstraction from a 19th-century tool quietly became the infrastructure of how humanity finds information, and it opens a door into why eigenvectors keep appearing everywhere from quantum mechanics to facial recognition.
